g.data-narration: Spanning Trees are essential graph theory concepts used in network design, connecting all vertices with exactly n-1 minimum edges for optimal connectivity.
g.text: Spanning Trees and Minimum Spanning Trees

g.data-narration: A spanning tree connects all vertices in a graph without creating cycles, containing exactly n-1 edges for n vertices in the network.
g.text: Spanning Tree Definition
• Connected: Path exists between every pair of vertices
• Acyclic: Contains no cycles
• Includes all vertices: Every vertex from original graph

g.data-narration: For n vertices, a spanning tree always has exactly n-1 edges. Complete graphs with n vertices have n^(n-2) distinct spanning trees as per Cayley's formula.
g.text: Key Properties
• Edges: Always exactly n-1 edges for n vertices
• Cayley's Formula: Complete graph K_n has n^(n-2) spanning trees
• Cut Property: Minimum edge crossing any cut belongs to some MST

g.data-narration: A Minimum Spanning Tree minimizes total edge weight in a weighted graph. The MST is guaranteed to be unique when all edge weights are distinct.
g.text: Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)
• Weighted graph: Edges have weights (costs, distances)
• MST: Spanning tree with minimum total edge weight
• Unique when all edge weights are distinct

g.data-narration: Kruskal's algorithm sorts all edges by weight and adds them greedily if they connect different components, using Union-Find data structure to detect cycles efficiently.
g.text: Kruskal's Algorithm
1. Sort all edges by weight (ascending)
2. Use Union-Find to add edges that connect different sets
3. Stop when (n-1) edges added | Time: O(E log V)

g.data-narration: Prim's algorithm grows the MST from an arbitrary starting vertex by repeatedly adding the minimum weight edge that connects the tree to a new vertex.
g.text: Prim's Algorithm
1. Start from arbitrary vertex
2. At each step, add minimum weight edge connecting MST to outside vertex
3. Repeat until all vertices included | Time: O(V²) basic, O(E log V) with PQ

g.data-narration: Kruskal works better for sparse graphs while Prim excels in dense graphs. Both are greedy algorithms with applications in network design, telecommunications, and circuit routing.
g.text: Comparison & Applications
• Kruskal: Better for sparse graphs | Prim: Better for dense graphs
• Both greedy algorithms guarantee optimal MST (Cut Property)
• Applications: Network design, telephone lines, airline routes, circuit design
